cell.rationale: A clear single clause governs the act — the retrieved Law states that a goal is scored when the whole of the ball passes over the goal line, between the goalposts and under the crossbar, provided that no offence has been committed by the team scoring the goal (ifab-law10-goal-scored-p97). This clause provides a clear rule for determining when a goal is scored. There are no competing clauses or silence on decisive elements.

Nothing makes the truth unrecoverable — the retrieved facts state there was no goal-line technology or VAR in 2010, and the referee along with the assistant had to judge in real time whether the ball had crossed the line without any review available (lampard-hist-no-glt-2010).

The retrieved facts state there was no goal-line technology or VAR in 2010, and the referee along with the assistant had to judge in real time whether the ball had crossed the line without any review available (lampard-hist-no-glt-2010). Additionally, neither official had a clear view of where the ball landed as they were not level with the goal line at the moment of the bounce (lampard-hist-sightline). The ball struck the crossbar and bounced down well behind the goal line before spinning back out (lampard-hist-crossed-line), which television replays within seconds confirmed had clearly crossed the line. Therefore, the decisive truth was not available to the officials at the moment of the decision due to a lack of review technology and obstructed sightlines, constituting a decision-time deficit.

Named sources are one-sided — frank Lampard (lampard-framing-lampard) condemns the non-award of the goal as a mistake that changed the match's course, while Sepp Blatter (lampard-framing-blatter) admits the error and calls for goal-line technology to prevent such incidents.

ifab-law10-goal-scored-p97.extracted_text: A goal is scored when the whole of the ball passes over the goal line, between the goalposts and under the crossbar, provided that no offence has been committed by the team scoring the goal.

lampard-framing-blatter.extracted_text: Sepp Blatter (2010-06-29) — ADMITS: Blatter publicly apologised for the error and reopened the goal-line technology debate, treating the missed call as a factual mistake to be fixed, not a contested judgement.

lampard-framing-lampard.extracted_text: Frank Lampard (2010-06-27) — CONDEMNS: Lampard said there was no doubt the ball was over the line and that the decision not to award the goal changed the course of the match.

lampard-framing-merkel-context.attribution: named commentary at the time, contemporary German match coverage (2010-06-27)
lampard-framing-merkel-context.extracted_text: named commentary at the time (2010-06-27) — ADMITS: German coverage and players widely acknowledged the ball had crossed the line and that the goal should have stood — agreement on the fact, not division over it.

lampard-hist-crossed-line.attribution: Curated historical record — Lampard's ghost goal
lampard-hist-crossed-line.extracted_text: Frank Lampard's shot struck the crossbar and bounced down well behind the goal line before spinning back out. Television replays available within seconds showed the whole ball had clearly crossed the line.

lampard-hist-no-glt-2010.attribution: Curated historical record — Lampard's ghost goal
lampard-hist-no-glt-2010.extracted_text: In 2010 there was no goal-line technology and no VAR. The referee and assistant had to judge in real time whether the ball had crossed the line, with no review available; the goal was not given.

lampard-hist-sightline.attribution: Curated historical record — Lampard's ghost goal
lampard-hist-sightline.extracted_text: The ball was in and out of the goal in a fraction of a second and neither official was level with the goal line at the moment of the bounce, so neither had a clear view of where it landed.

output.rationale: The retrieved Law states that a goal is scored when the whole of the ball passes over the goal line, between the goalposts and under the crossbar, provided that no offence has been committed by the team scoring the goal (ifab-law10-goal-scored-p97). This clause provides a clear rule for determining when a goal is scored. There are no competing clauses or silence on decisive elements; hence, there is no rule ambiguity.
No evidence was retrieved for this lens on this incident.

The retrieved facts state there was no goal-line technology or VAR in 2010, and the referee along with the assistant had to judge in real time whether the ball had crossed the line without any review available (lampard-hist-no-glt-2010). Additionally, neither official had a clear view of where the ball landed as they were not level with the goal line at the moment of the bounce (lampard-hist-sightline). The ball struck the crossbar and bounced down well behind the goal line before spinning back out (lampard-hist-crossed-line), which television replays within seconds confirmed had clearly crossed the line. Therefore, the decisive truth was not available to the officials at the moment of the decision due to a lack of review technology and obstructed sightlines, constituting a decision-time deficit.

Frank Lampard (lampard-framing-lampard) condemns the non-award of the goal as a mistake that changed the match's course, while Sepp Blatter (lampard-framing-blatter) admits the error and calls for goal-line technology to prevent such incidents. German coverage and players (lampard-framing-merkel-context) also acknowledged the ball had crossed the line, indicating agreement on the fact, not division over it.

settled_fact.statement: Frank Lampard's shot struck the crossbar and bounced down well behind the goal line against Germany in the 2010 World Cup. The goal was not given, England lost, and replays showed the whole ball had clearly crossed the line. That the ball crossed the line is not in dispute.

split.headline: It stays contested over what could be seen in the moment — not over the dimensions ruled out.
